title: The Wrong Direction
subtitle: The main ways strategy goes wrong

hook.metaphor: There are only so many ways to steer a ship onto the rocks: misread the chart, ignore a faster vessel overtaking you, take on a leaky ship in a merger, or sail boldly into a port with no harbour. Strategic failures rhyme — and learning their shapes means you can spot one forming before it's fatal.
hook.scenario: A taxi giant dismissed a small ride-hailing app as a toy. A retailer bought a trendy brand for a fortune that turned out to be worthless. A bank charged into a foreign market it didn't understand and lost a fortune. Three different companies, three classic strategic mistakes. Can you name the patterns?

learn: **Disruption / obsolescence:** a new technology or business model makes your offering irrelevant. The taxi giant dismissing the ride-hailing app is the textbook case — incumbents routinely under-rate disruptors precisely because they look small and crude at first (Kodak/digital, Blockbuster/streaming).

**Failed M&A (mergers & acquisitions):** buying another company that turns out overpriced, broken, or impossible to integrate. Our retailer's worthless trendy brand is a classic over-paid acquisition — a huge share of mergers destroy value rather than create it.

**Bad market entry:** charging into a new geography, segment, or product you don't truly understand — our bank's foreign misadventure. Confidence from success at home often blinds firms to how different the new market really is.

**Missing or misjudging competitors and customers:** failing to see a rival's move, or a shift in what customers want, until it's too late. Strategy is relative — you can do everything 'right' and still lose because someone else changed the game.

**Concentration / over-reliance:** betting too much on one customer, product, market, or assumption, so a single change is catastrophic (the concentration idea, at the strategic level). The common thread across all of these: strategic failures come from **misreading change** — and they're survivable mostly if spotted early.
bridge: So at work, you can scan for the classic strategic traps instead of being blindsided by them: *Are we dismissing a 'toy' disruptor? Over-paying for an acquisition? Charging into a market we don't understand? Missing a competitor or customer shift? Over-reliant on one bet?* These patterns repeat across decades — recognising the shape early is what turns a fatal strategic error into a course correction.

interactions.title: Name the strategic failure
interactions.prompt: Match each scenario to the strategic-failure pattern it shows.

pairs.left: Overpaying for a brand that proves worthless
Charging into a market you don't understand
Betting the firm on a single big client

interactions.title: 'It's just a toy'
interactions.prompt: Executives dismiss a small, clunky competitor as no threat. What's the wise stance?

options.label: Agree — it's tiny and crude, so ignore it.
options.feedback: This is exactly how incumbents get disrupted. Disruptors almost always start small and crude — that's the disguise. 'It's just a toy' is the famous last words of obsolete giants.

options.label: Take it seriously — disruptors usually start looking like toys.
options.feedback: Exactly. The pattern repeats across history: the threat looks negligible until it isn't. Watching small disruptors closely is how you avoid the obsolescence trap.

options.label: Panic and copy it instantly.
options.feedback: Taking it seriously is right; blind panic-copying isn't. You study the threat and respond deliberately — neither dismiss it nor flail at it.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Strategy goes wrong in repeating patterns — disruption you dismissed, acquisitions you overpaid for, markets you didn't understand, competitors you missed, and bets you over-concentrated — and they're survivable mainly if you spot the shape early.”

checkpoint.q: Why do incumbents so often get disrupted?
options: Disruptors start small and crude, so they're easy to dismiss as 'toys'
checkpoint.explain: The threat looks negligible at first — that disguise is exactly why it gets under-rated until too late.

checkpoint.q: Failed M&A is a strategic risk because…
options: Many acquisitions destroy value — overpriced, broken, or un-integrable
It has nothing to do with strategy
checkpoint.explain: A large share of mergers destroy rather than create value — a classic big-bet strategic mistake.

checkpoint.q: The common thread across strategic failures is…
options: Misreading change — and they're survivable mainly if spotted early
checkpoint.explain: Disruption, bad entries, missed competitors — all are failures to read change in time.
